charles_bridge.address: Between Old Town and Malá Strana over the Vltava
charles_bridge.description: A medieval stone bridge lined with statues, now a pedestrian spine between the castle side and the Old Town. Early mornings and late evenings are quieter for crossing.
charles_bridge.history: Founded in 1357 under Charles IV; the Baroque sculptures were added from the late 17th century onward. The bridge has been repaired after floods and military action.
charles_bridge.significance: The iconic image of Prague in travel literature and a daily route for residents as well as visitors.
facts: The bridge is about 516 metres long and rests on 16 arches.
Many statues are 17th–18th century copies; originals are partly in the National Museum.

prague_castle.description: A fortified hilltop complex: palaces, churches, and gardens overlooking the city. St. Vitus Cathedral dominates the skyline from many viewpoints.
prague_castle.history: The site has been a seat of power since the 9th century; major phases include Gothic, Renaissance, and Baroque rebuilding under successive rulers.
prague_castle.significance: Historic residence of Bohemian kings and the modern Czech president; UNESCO-listed with related urban zones.
facts: The Guinness Book once listed it as the largest ancient castle complex in the world (by area).
Golden Lane is a picturesque row of small houses inside the fortifications.

old_town_square.description: The main square of the Old Town: Týn Church, the Old Town Hall with the astronomical clock, cafés, and seasonal markets.
old_town_square.history: The square grew from a market place; public executions, coronation processions, and modern protests have all left their mark on its memory.
old_town_square.significance: Social and ceremonial heart of the historic centre and a starting point for many walking routes.
facts: The Jan Hus monument (1915) anchors the centre of the square.
Christmas and Easter markets draw large crowds here.

astronomical_clock_orloj.address: Old Town Hall, Old Town Square
astronomical_clock_orloj.description: A medieval clock with an astronomical dial, apostles in the windows, and the famous skeleton figure (“Death”) ringing the bell.
astronomical_clock_orloj.history: The mechanism dates from 1410 (astronomer Jan Šindel and clockmaker Mikuláš of Kadaň); later layers added the calendar and figurine show.
astronomical_clock_orloj.significance: One of the oldest operating astronomical clocks and a symbol of Prague craftsmanship.
facts: Crowds gather on the hour to watch the apostles’ procession.
The clock was damaged in 1945 and restored in the following years.
astronomical_clock_orloj.architecture_style: Gothic clock integrated into the town hall

st_vitus_cathedral.description: The metropolitan cathedral of Prague: soaring nave, stained glass including Alfons Mucha’s window, and the St. Wenceslas Chapel.
st_vitus_cathedral.history: Construction began in 1344 under Charles IV; the cathedral was only finished in the 19th and early 20th centuries with Neo-Gothic completion work.
st_vitus_cathedral.significance: Coronation church of Bohemian kings and the burial place of several saints and rulers.
facts: The Great South Tower can be climbed for a view over the city.
The crown jewels were historically kept in the Crown Jewels chamber.

wenceslas_square.description: A long boulevard-like square with the National Museum at its upper end and the equestrian statue of St. Wenceslas below.
wenceslas_square.history: Named and reshaped in the 19th century; site of key moments in 20th-century Czech history including 1968 and 1989 gatherings.
wenceslas_square.significance: Commercial artery and traditional place for public demonstrations and national holidays.
facts: Despite the name, it reads more like a wide avenue than a compact square.
The National Museum façade was restored in the 2010s.

old_town_hall.description: Gothic tower and administrative complex; the astronomical clock is on its southern face. The tower offers views over the red roofs of the Old Town.
old_town_hall.history: Origins in the 14th century; the Gothic tower and chapel are the most prominent surviving medieval parts after wartime damage and rebuilds.
old_town_hall.significance: Seat of Old Town self-government for centuries and a landmark silhouette on the square.
facts: Parts of the building can be visited on guided routes including the underground.
The chapel interior includes notable Gothic vaulting.

old_jewish_cemetery.description: Layered gravestones crowded on a small plot—one of Europe’s oldest surviving Jewish burial grounds, now part of the Jewish Museum circuit.
old_jewish_cemetery.history: Used from the early 15th century until 1787; because space was tight, earth was added and stones overlap in depth.
old_jewish_cemetery.significance: A poignant record of the Prague Jewish community before modern urban clearance and Holocaust losses.
facts: Rabbi Loew (Maharal) is traditionally associated with burials in this ground.
Access is usually with a combined ticket to nearby synagogues.

powder_gate.description: A Gothic gate-tower that was once one of the entrances to the Old Town; decorative stonework rises above pedestrian traffic.
powder_gate.history: Built in the 15th century; used for gunpowder storage at one time, hence the name. Linked visually to the route toward the Old Town Square.
powder_gate.significance: Landmark boundary between Old and New Town and a backdrop for coronation processions of old.
facts: You can climb the tower for a view along Celetná.
It stands near the Municipal House and the modern shopping district.

municipal_house.description: Art Nouveau concert and civic hall: façade mosaics, Mucha-decorated interiors in places, and the main home of the Prague Symphony Orchestra.
municipal_house.history: Built 1905–1912 on the site of the former royal court; the Czechoslovak independence was declared here in 1918.
municipal_house.significance: Prime example of Czech Secession architecture and a working performance venue.
facts: Guided tours show representative rooms when concerts allow.
The building combines café, exhibition spaces, and the Smetana Hall.

national_museum_prague.description: Neoclassical museum building at the head of Wenceslas Square; collections span natural sciences, history, and arts.
national_museum_prague.history: Founded in 1818; the dominant historical building was completed in the 1890s and underwent major renovation in the 2010s.
national_museum_prague.significance: Flagship state museum and architectural anchor of the square.
facts: A new building and underground link connect across the tram underpass.
The dome and staircase are favourites for interior photography.

petrin_tower.description: A steel lookout tower inspired by the Eiffel Tower, smaller but placed high on Petřín hill for sweeping views over Prague.
petrin_tower.history: Built for the 1891 Jubilee Exhibition; carried up the hill by funicular or footpaths.
petrin_tower.significance: Favourite panorama point and a recognizable silhouette from the city below.
facts: About 299 steps lead to the main viewing platform.
The funicular from Újezd is part of the public transport network.

lennon_wall.description: A wall covered in graffiti, lyrics, and messages—once an unofficial memorial after John Lennon’s death, now repainted freely by visitors.
lennon_wall.history: In the 1980s youth wrote protest lyrics here under communist censorship; ownership and repainting cycles continue today.
lennon_wall.significance: Symbol of free expression in Cold War Prague and a tourist stop near Kampa.
facts: The wall is on ground belonging to the Sovereign Military Order of Malta.
Content changes constantly; photos date quickly.

dancing_house.description: A deconstructivist office building by Frank Gehry and Vlado Milunić, nicknamed “Fred and Ginger” for its paired curved volumes.
dancing_house.history: Completed in 1996 on a site cleared by WWII bombing; controversial at first, now a postcard favourite.
dancing_house.significance: Represents post-1989 architectural openness on the riverside.
facts: A glass bar on the top offers views toward the castle.
It stands opposite the river from the National Theatre area.

vysehrad_ss_peter_paul.description: Neo-Gothic basilica with striking twin spires inside the Vyšehrad fort—associated with the Czech national cemetery Slavín nearby.
vysehrad_ss_peter_paul.history: Older church on site; present form largely 19th-century reconstruction with Art Nouveau interior painting.
vysehrad_ss_peter_paul.significance: Vyšehrad is a legendary “first Prague castle” in medieval chronicles and a quiet park above the river.
facts: Composers such as Dvořák are buried in the adjacent cemetery.
Ramparts offer views of the Vltava and New Town.

strahov_monastery.description: Premonstratensian monastery with a famous library hall and church towers visible from Petřín and the castle approaches.
strahov_monastery.history: Founded in the 12th century; library and theological collections grew through the early modern period.
strahov_monastery.significance: Important manuscript collections and Baroque library interiors (visiting rules vary).
facts: The monastery brewery has a long tradition and a popular restaurant.
Nearby Petřín paths connect to the castle gardens.

kampa_charles_bridge_view.description: Park-like island by the river: willows, museum of modern art at one end, and classic views back toward the Charles Bridge arches.
kampa_charles_bridge_view.history: Former mills and gardens; gradually landscaped for public leisure from the 20th century.
kampa_charles_bridge_view.significance: Calmer riverside space minutes from the dense tourist core.
facts: The Čertovka side canal is nicknamed “Prague’s Venice”.
Outdoor sculptures include the famous babies by David Černý on nearby lawns.

prague_main_railway_station.description: Art Nouveau terminal behind a historic façade; the glass dome of the new hall is visible from the street approach.
prague_main_railway_station.history: Opened in its present form in the early 20th century; later modern additions handle high-speed and international traffic.
prague_main_railway_station.significance: Main rail gateway to the city and an interior worth a quick look even if you are not travelling.
facts: Original Fanta building interiors were restored for public access.
Metro line C connects directly underground.
prague_main_railway_station.architecture_style: Art Nouveau terminal with modern halls

st_nicholas_mala_strana.description: Baroque church with a pale green dome and rich interior stucco; organ concerts are frequent in the tourist season.
st_nicholas_mala_strana.history: Built in the 18th century on the site of a Gothic predecessor; associated with Jesuit patronage.
st_nicholas_mala_strana.significance: One of the finest Baroque church interiors in Prague and a dominant of Malá Strana square.
facts: Do not confuse with the other St. Nicholas in the Old Town.
The bell tower can sometimes be climbed on tours.

troja_castle.description: Baroque château with formal terraced gardens stepping down toward the Vltava; Prague Zoo is just uphill.
troja_castle.history: Built for the Sternberg family in the late 17th century; garden statuary and staircases follow Italianate models.
troja_castle.significance: Popular half-day trip combining palace interiors, gardens, and zoo or botanic garden.
facts: The Grand Staircase aligns views with the river axis.
Temporary art exhibitions often use the staterooms.
troja_castle.architecture_style: Czech High Baroque palace and garden

church_1_prague.description: A text-only church entry added for itinerary balance and slower walking routes.
facts: Text-only entry to keep PDF builds stable.
Can be replaced with a sourced church later.
church_1_prague.history: Added as a structured placeholder with substantive text (no image).
church_1_prague.significance: Helps diversify the guide with more sacred architecture stops.
